signals.headline: IRAN WAR DRIVES HEADLINE INFLATION TO 3
signals.raw: IRAN WAR DRIVES HEADLINE INFLATION TO 3.3% IN MARCH, HIGHEST SPIKE SINCE 2022 | YES | HIGH confidence

signals.headline: Recent confirmation:** Headline CPI inflation surged to 3
signals.raw: Recent confirmation:** Headline CPI inflation surged to 3.29% year-over-year in March 2026, with a sharp 0.87% monthly gain[1], marking the largest monthly increase since 2022[2]. This represents a significant jump from February's 2.4%[3].

signals.headline: Market-moving catalyst:** The spike is directly attributable to the **Iran war**, which sent oil prices skyrocketing 45%
signals.raw: Market-moving catalyst:** The spike is directly attributable to the **Iran war**, which sent oil prices skyrocketing 45% in a single month—from $71 to $103 per barrel[1]. Gas prices exceeded $4 per gallon[2], creating immediate consumer impact.

signals.headline: Core CPI rose to 2
signals.raw: Core CPI rose to 2.6% year-over-year[2], showing some price pressure beyond just energy, though core PCE (0.2% monthly) came in slightly better than expected[2].

signals.headline: Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percentage point since the war began[1]
signals.raw: Goldman Sachs raised its December 2026 headline PCE inflation forecast by a full percentage point since the war began[1].

signals.headline: Short-term inflation expectations jumped sharply: University of Michigan survey rose to 4
signals.raw: Short-term inflation expectations jumped sharply: University of Michigan survey rose to 4.8%, and financial market-implied expectations for 2026 headline CPI rose 0.8 percentage points to 3.3%[1].

signals.headline: Forward guidance shift:** Despite the headline spike, Goldman forecasts core inflation easing later in 2026 as tariff pa
signals.raw: Forward guidance shift:** Despite the headline spike, Goldman forecasts core inflation easing later in 2026 as tariff passthrough completes, potentially enabling Fed rate cuts[1]. However, geopolitical risks remain the dominant wildcard over monetary policy[1].
